sentences.text: They took us on the selection.
They put them in a row, and the Germans were standing with the--
Not guns, with the rifles towards us.
I was standing next to my mother.
It was full of us.
And a woman who was standing with a child.
She suffocate her child to be able to go through the selection and throw it away.
It was a small child.
It was so moving.
And you saw some people on one side with children, older people, and some people what they thought would be able to work free for them on the other side.
When we came back to the factory, to the place where we were living, it was Erev Yom Kippur.
And people were calling that they should come for Kol Nidre.
And And they said, what?
We should go to ask God for forgiveness for all the atrocities what they've done to us?
We've got to forgive God for all this what he's done to us.
We don't have to pray to him.
It was terrible.
People were crying, shouting.
They lost wives, children, mothers, fathers, brothers.
And I lost, at the time, my brother.
At that time.
At that time.
A day before the selection, they took him to the Umschlagplatz, and my father couldn't get him out.
And they send them out, probably where I don't know.
Yes, we knew that we lost him.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We still were working there in the factory.
But after this selection, they stopped in the Warsaw Ghetto [? catching. ?] They wanted the people to work for them, that they should have much more.
They wanted the staff they needed.
Yes, my mother and my father.
But my uncle worked in the [NON-ENGLISH].
He used to do the odd jobs.
He used to-- but funny enough, whatever they told him to do, they used to do differently.
They never have done it like they told him to do.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then my father one day-- we knew that my aunts were in Vegrow.
And one day, a man came who was a friend of ours.
And he told us that the two aunts with a child were killed in Vegrow.
They were in hiding in the basement, and they couldn't catch the bread.
They went up on the attic, and the Germans came there.
My auntie had a gold bracelet to get off to give it to him.
He shot her.
My other aunt starts screaming, and the child, and they shot all three.
When I found out this and they told me, I was a young girl, and I fainted.
I loved very much my aunts.
And it was a very moving moment in my life.
It was in 1942.
I was 18.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Then my father said that life-- but my little cousin, we send out the night before the selection with a lady to be outside the ghetto.
No.
They put them there to a house, and they told that they will be able to get out of the ghetto.
But unfortunately, the Polish police caught them.
And the Polack came to us, and he told us that it has to cost so and so much money to let them free, that we paid the money.
And she was with this Polish family.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And my father decided that I should go out from the ghetto.
Then we once used to have a maid, and her name was Stefania Skwierczynska.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And we had her birth certificate.
I don't know how.
And I left the ghetto under the name Stefania Skwierczynska.
Yes.
You see, I went-- I pretend that I am going to work.
Because some Jews were going, and they went out from the ghetto for work, and then they came back in the evening.
And I went out with them.
It was dark, in the morning.
And then I took off my armband, and I went to the place where I was supposed to go.
And I went there, and I went outside towards the-- I was with a little girl and with an old woman.
And one day--
Who?
No, that old woman was a Christian.
Because her son-in-law, my father knew.
And she said she will look after me.
And I went there, and this woman was a wicked old cow.
And one day-- she used to leave us for the whole day, sometimes for two days.
And one day, in the morning came some German and they-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] ask me for my papers.
I showed them the papers.
And they told me I should tell them the Polish prayer.
Funny enough, they taught me to learn the Polish prayer.
And I said the Polish prayer, and they left me.
And I got scared.
I saw that this old woman-- and I went back to the ghetto.
My father was very upset when I came back to the ghetto.
But I couldn't help myself.
I didn't want to be there because this woman was wicked.
And we tried now to prepare places that we should be able to leave the ghetto and to be in hiding in proper place.
When my father had to shop at the [POLISH], there used to be a caretaker over the road.
And this caretaker's name was [NON-ENGLISH].
And my uncle, who used to be, before the war, the bodyguard of Marshall Pilsudzki and Rydz [NON-ENGLISH], when he didn't look Jewish, he was the first one to go out.
And he arranged with her to buy two flats, to furnish them, and to make a hiding place in the flats.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Then my other uncle went out.
Arnold.
But the first which went out was [NON-ENGLISH].
[NON-ENGLISH].
The other one was Arnold Zabielak, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and he went out.
And I left the ghetto again on the 1st of February, 1943.
By the same method.
I went out, and I went to the place there, to the [POLISH].
And then the flat was organized.
It was like a fortress.
It was a very big block of flats.
It was actually three, but the entrance was from number one.
And our flat was number five, you know, in the house number five.
It was on the second floor.
And we had there a room, the last room in that flat.
And they had there not a-- a cupboard, and the cupboard-- on the cupboard, they made a wall with-- on the bottom, there was with shelves.
It was like a-- like a cupboard, you know?
But this one in the back, it was-- A false?
No, it was a little place, you know, where you could put in all different kinds of stuff.
But it was a bigger cupboard.
Behind it.
Then they partition it.
They make like a cupboard with shelves, and on the shelves they put on all different kinds of saucepans and so on.
But on the bottom, they made a frame.
The frame was filled up with bricks.
This you pulled away, and on the stomach you went in there.
And from the place, from that-- because when somebody came to a caretaker to find out where somebody is-- then from there to our flat was a bell.
In case the Germans are coming, then they put on the hand on the coat, and there was the bell.
And the bell rang by us, and then we knew we've got to go--
--into our hiding place.
One day--
It was quite-- it was like a little room.
One day, the woman first-- and then we had a third flat, which was furnished.
It was on the name of that wicked woman what I told you.
And I was with my-- with Arnold in that flat.
But in the other flat, my mother was with [NON-ENGLISH].
Yes.
No, she left later.
And we had already all the flats prepared.
But one flat what we prepared-- because then the Germans said if they will find somebody by a Pole, the Pole would be killed together with the Jews.
Then this Pole decided not to have anybody, and he took away the flat.
We, both the flat, furnished it, and they took it away.
No, one.
We had three, and one he took away.
No.
This one with the cupboard was-- then my mother was there with [NON-ENGLISH].
And they couldn't-- no.
It was like this.
We were in that flat.
And in the meantime, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] the uprising started in the Warsaw Ghetto, and my father was behind the wall.
A friend of mine knocked out the wall with a hand grenade.
And he came-- I don't know how he knew, he came to that woman, and he said that my father is alive.
Yes.
That Pole, what he was a friend of my father, he had  a friend, an SS man.
And this SS man was a rather nice man.
For money, he would do anything.
And he said he would go to the ghetto to bring my father, but he can't go at night because the Jews are shooting at night.
But can you imagine?
When it start, the uprising in the ghetto, it started before Pesach.
And I was standing behind the curtain watching, burning the ghetto.
Can you imagine our hearts when we were there, and we saw the ghetto is burning, and we couldn't do nothing to help?
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

But I forgot to tell you that when we were-- before the ghetto start burning, [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] my father used to collect money in the ghetto, send out, and they used to send guns and ammunition into the ghetto.
Because the-- you know, those-- those fellows which collected the-- the German uniforms, they came with a cart with horses.
And in the cart, they put it in.
And they bought pumps and all different-- that people should hide in the ghetto, you know, underneath, and my husband used to organize it all.
He wasn't my husband then.
He was my uncle.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then when the ghetto start burning, they were shooting.
And my friend then came out of the ghetto.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [LAUGHS] The German said that he will go, if that young man would stay there overnight, and collect my father.
Then he will go, leave in-- in the afternoon, and the next afternoon he will come to collect them both.
My friend was left in the ghetto.
And when he went to the place where my father was, they told him my father is dead.
Because in the morning, the Germans used to come to burn the houses.
Then my father went with a friend on top of the building.
And when the Germans arrived, they had hand grenades, and they throw, and the lorry with the Germans exploded.
But one of the Germans saw it, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you know, was saved.
And when they came down, he shot them.
Then my friend already heard that my father is dead.
And when he came back and he told us this, can you imagine how I felt?
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My mother was in the other flat, and I was in  the other flat.
I went to see my mother, knowing I haven't got a father.
You had to tell her that, did you?
No, she knew it.
She was sitting shiva there.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] How did she sit shiva?
On the bed without shawl.
And I was sitting with her.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And when I was coming back, to the other flat where I was, I was caught by the Poles, and I was blackmailed.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And I came back.
And then [INAUDIBLE] the people which had bought the flat what we had there-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the woman from whom they bought the flat, she said that she left something in the cupboard.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] she came to look for this thing what she left in the cupboard.
And this cupboard was closed in.
She said, what have you done here?
This cupboard was fantastic.
We could put in-- I had such a-- it was so useful for me.
Why did you close it up?
Then the husband of the house, of that woman, came up, and he said, I'm sorry you've got to leave.
And he broke down this-- The back of the cupboard.
--the back of the cupboard.
He said, it is not good.
And there was a very big oven, a [INAUDIBLE] oven, what they used to warm the room.
Then my husband said to him, you know what?
We will hide in that oven.
No, to the--
--to the Polish man.
He said, we will empty the oven, because there is a lot of rubbish in it.
We will put in two big [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] cots.
And on the one cot, we will put on a little chair.
And on the top, we will make a metal frame, fill it up with-- [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] with other [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] tiles, with little tiles.
And we will have it on hinges, and we will open and we will go in there with a ladder.
You will take away the ladder, and there we will hide.
One day, it happened again.
And their oven was connected to this oven where we were in hiding.
And all the flames were coming from us.
We couldn't breathe when we went into hiding.
Then in that block was a big attic.
And on that attic, they used to keep, because they belonged to the [NON-ENGLISH], to the Polish organization, you know, the socialist organization.
Then they said [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that, we will make smaller this attic, and you will be able to get in on the stomach as well, and it will be all right.
And at that time, we brought my mother and my uncle, and all of us were in that place.
Where?
Into the oven to hide.
If there was an alarm, we had to go to the oven.
It was the bell was ringing from her room to us.
From the caretaker.
Then we knew that the police or somebody is coming to the flat.
Depends.
No.
It was sometimes once a week, sometimes twice a week.
Depends.
If somebody was coming up, we had to go there and that's it, that nobody should see us.
And funny enough, that woman, every time she used to come to us to give her money.
Then one day she said, we should sit and make cigarettes.
Then we were sitting and making cigarettes.
She bought tobacco for our money.
We were manufacturing the cigarettes she used to sell.
She used to take the money to the pocket.
But we didn't mind, as long as they were not nasty to us.
Before this, my father send out some clothes for us and some other things.
And that Pole what was friendly with my father brought this sack of this stuff to the caretaker for us.
You know what she have done?
They took it all away.
And they told-- because I brought him some stockings out of the ghetto when I came.
And she came that I pinched her stockings, you know?
Because they wanted to take us all away from us.
And they took away the whole sack with this stuff.
